narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 43% chance that you would fail it—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data about past students' academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and study commitments, helping us identify those most likely to succeed in law school and beyond. In your case, the strongest factor pushing toward this higher failure risk was your plan to attend part-time, as the vast majority of students who passed the bar—over 93%—were full-time students, and part-time status often correlates with added challenges in maintaining the intensive focus needed for bar success. On a more positive note, your third-year decile ranking stood out as a key strength, placing you solidly in the 7th decile compared to the 5.91 average among bar passers, which helped pull the prediction toward success by showing your ability to perform well later in your studies. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 exceeded the 3.24 average of those who passed, acting as another favorable influence that tempered the overall risk assessment. While your LSAT score of 40 was above the passers' average of 37.27 and your upper-middle family income aligned well with typical successful applicants, these didn't outweigh the dominant impact of your part-time choice. Your first-year decile of 7 also compared favorably to the 6.04 average for passers, reflecting solid early performance. Overall, the model saw your profile as promising in academics but weighed the part-time commitment most heavily against you, unlike the full-time paths of most admitted students who went on to pass. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many factors like personal drive play a role beyond any model. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan, as that single change could significantly shift your predicted odds, or strengthen other areas like further test prep if part-time remains your path. You might also seek feedback from advisors on bolstering late-stage academic momentum, building on your third-year success. With targeted adjustments, a future application could look much brighter.
